title: Govt of India Act, 1935
intro: “The Government of India Act, 1935 was India's constitutional rehearsal without sovereignty.”
“Designed to perpetuate imperial control, the Government of India Act, 1935 instead became the blueprint for India's constitutional democracy.”

head: Feature
Explanation
Significance
rows: ==All-India Federation==
Proposed a Federation of India of British Indian provinces and princely states — to come into being only after sufficient princely states acceded, which never happened.
First formal attempt to give India a ==federal constitutional structure==.
==Three-fold division of powers==
Legislative subjects divided into ==Federal, Provincial and Concurrent Lists==; federal law prevailed on repugnancy in the concurrent field.
Direct ancestor of the ==Union, State and Concurrent Lists== of the Constitution.
Provincial autonomy
Dyarchy abolished in provinces; elected ministries could function in provincial subjects.
Real experience of running governments before independence — especially after the ==1937 provincial elections==.
Dyarchy at the Centre
Provincial dyarchy ended, but dyarchy was introduced at the Centre; ==defence and external affairs== stayed under the Governor-General's direct control.
Showed the Act's contradiction: autonomy below, imperial control above.
Bicameralism
A federal legislature with two chambers was contemplated; some provinces also had bicameral legislatures.
Influenced bicameralism in Parliament and some State legislatures.
==Federal Court==
Original jurisdiction over disputes between the Federation, provinces and federated states; appellate jurisdiction on interpretation of the Act.
Institutional predecessor of the ==Supreme Court of India==.
Public Service Commissions
Provided for a Federal PSC and Provincial PSCs.
Framework influenced ==Articles 315–323== of the Constitution.
Expanded franchise
Electorate expanded from about ==3% to about 14%== of the population.
Widened political participation, though far from universal adult suffrage.
Separate electorates
Continued for Muslims, Sikhs and others; communal representation reflected in provincial legislature composition.
Deepened communal political identities; criticised by nationalists.
Emergency & reserve powers
Governors and the Governor-General retained special responsibilities, discretionary powers and emergency authority.
Responsible government remained heavily controlled by colonial officials.
Ordinance power
The Governor-General could promulgate ordinances when the legislature was not in session.
Colonial antecedent of ==ordinance-making power== in independent India.
Reserve Bank of India context
==RBI began operations on 1 April 1935== under the RBI Act, 1934; the framework recognised modern financial governance needs.
Strengthened centralised monetary and fiscal administration.

head: Colonial Act, 1935
Constitution of India, 1950
rows: Sovereignty vested in the ==British Crown==.
Sovereignty vested in ==“We, the People of India”==.
Limited franchise.
==Universal adult suffrage==.
Communal electorates.
Common electoral roll, with affirmative representation through reservations.
Governor-General/Governors had real discretionary colonial power.
President/Governors are generally constitutional heads.
No justiciable Fundamental Rights.
==Fundamental Rights enforceable by courts==.
Federation subject to princely accession and imperial control.
Union of India based on constitutional integration.
Police-state orientation.
==Welfare-state orientation== through Fundamental Rights and Directive Principles.

blocks.text: The Constitution did not simply “copy” the 1935 Act — it ==democratised, nationalised and moralised== it.

items: Three lists became the foundation of Indian legislative federalism
==Strong Centre bias== was inherited and strengthened
Provincial autonomy was the seed of State governments
The Federal Court became the model for a ==federal constitutional umpire==
Emergency centralisation entered Indian federal imagination

blocks.text: The Government of India Act, 1935 gave India the grammar of constitutional government, but not the soul of constitutional democracy.
